articles.title: Breaking News in 1776: How the Declaration of Independence Spread Across the Colonies
articles.summary: Imagine getting your news by word of mouth or a single printed sheet. That's how Americans first learned about the Declaration of Independence. A new book by historian Emily Sneff, *When the Declaration of Independence Was News*, reveals the fascinating journey of the document. Printed by John Dunlap on July 4, 1776, the Declaration was read aloud in town squares and reprinted in newspapers. But not everyone was thrilled—some were anxious about the future. The first copies didn't even include the signers' names. It wasn't until January 1777 that Mary Katharine Goddard printed a version with the names. This story shows how fragile and unpredictable news was 250 years ago.

articles.title: Alcaraz Out of French Open: Defending Champion Sidelined by Wrist Injury
articles.summary: Two-time defending champion Carlos Alcaraz will miss the French Open due to a wrist injury, he announced on Instagram. The 22-year-old world number two hurt his right wrist at the Barcelona Open and, after tests, decided to skip both the Italian Open and Roland Garros. Alcaraz won the Australian Open this year, becoming the youngest man to complete a career Grand Slam. His rival Jannik Sinner, now world number one, expressed sadness and hopes Alcaraz returns for Wimbledon. This opens the door for Sinner to win his first French Open and complete his own career Grand Slam.

articles.title: FDA Approves First Gene Therapy for Inherited Deafness: A 'Landmark Moment'
articles.summary: The FDA has approved Otarmeni, the first gene therapy for inherited deafness, targeting hearing loss caused by mutations in the OTOF gene. In a trial of 20 participants, 16 showed improved hearing within six months, with some able to hear whispers. The one-time treatment uses harmless viruses to deliver a working copy of the gene into the ear. Regeneron, the maker, says the therapy itself will be free to U.S. patients (excluding administration costs). Experts call it a "landmark moment" that could accelerate development of future genetic therapies for hearing loss.
